volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 4.3.1037 - Section 5.2.1145)

sections.label: Section 4.3.1037
sections.original: Blepharo , quaeso ut advocatus mi adsis neve abeas .
sections.translation: Blepharo , I pray that you ’ ll stay as my advocate , and not go away .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1037b
sections.original: Vale .
sections.translation: Farewell . What need is there of me for an advocate , who don ’ t know which of the two to side with ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.1039
sections.original: Intro ego hinc eo . Alcumena parturit .—
sections.translation: I ’ m going hence in-doors : Alcmena is in labour .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1039b
sections.original: Perii miser .
sections.translation: I ’ m undone , wretch that I am ;

sections.label: Section 4.3.1040
sections.original: quid ego faciam , quem advocati iam atque amici deserunt ?
sections.translation: for what am I to do , when my advocates and friends are now forsaking me ? Never , by heavens , shall he deride me unrevenged , whoever he is . Now will I betake myself straight to the king , and tell him of the matter as it has happened . By my faith , I will this day take vengeance on this Thessalian sorcerer , who has wrongfully distracted the minds of my household .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1045
sections.original: sed ubi illest ? intro edepol abiit , credo ad uxorem meam .
sections.translation: But where is he ? ( Looking around .) By my troth , he ’ s off into the house , to my wife , I suppose . What other person lives in Thebes more wretched than myself ? What now shall I do ? I , whom all men deny and deride just as they please . I am resolved ; I ’ ll burst into the house ; there , whatever person I perceive , whether maid-servant or man-servant , whether wife or whether paramour ,

sections.label: Section 4.3.1050
sections.original: seu patrem sive ávom videbo , óbtruncabo in aedibus .
sections.translation: whether father or whether grandfather , I ’ ll behead that person in the house ; neither Jupiter nor all the Deities shall hinder me from this , even if they would , but that I ’ ll do just as I have resolved .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1053
sections.original: Spes átque opes vitaé meae iacént sepultae in pectore ,
sections.translation: The hopes and resources of my life lie buried in my breast , nor is there any boldness in my heart , but what I have lost it .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1055
sections.original: ita mihi videntur omnia , mare terra caelum , consequi ,
sections.translation: So much to me do all things seem , the sea , the earth , the heavens , to be conspiring , that now I may be crushed , that I may be destroyed . Ah , wretched me ! I know not what to do . Prodigies so great have come to pass within the house . Ah ! woe is me ! I ’ m sick at heart , some water I could wish ! I ’ m overpowered and I ’ m utterly undone . My head is aching , and I cannot hear , nor do I see well with my eyes .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1060
sections.original: nec me miserior femina est neque ulla videatur magis .
sections.translation: No woman is there more wretched than myself , nor can one seem to be more so . Thus has it this day befallen my mistress ; for when she invoked for herself the Deities of travail , what rumblings and grumblings [*] , crashes and flashes ; suddenly , how instantaneously did it thunder , and how woundy loud . On the spot where each one stood , at the peal he fell ; then some one , I know not who , exclaimed in a mighty voice , Alcmena , succour is at hand , fear not :

sections.label: Section 5.1.1065
sections.original: et tibi et tuis propitius caeli cultor advenit .
sections.translation: propitious both to thee and thine , the Ruler of the Heavens comes . Arise , it said , ye who have fallen down in your terror through dread of me . As I lay , I arose ; I fancied that the house was in flames . Then Alcmena called me ; and then did that circumstance strike me with horror . Fears for my mistress took possession of me ; I ran to her to enquire what she wanted ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.1070
sections.original: atque illam geminos filios pueros peperisse conspicor ;
sections.translation: and then I beheld that she had given birth to two male children ; not yet did any one of us perceive when she was delivered , or indeed expect it . ( Sees AMPHITRYON .) But what ’ s this ? Who ’ s this old man that ’ s lying thus before our house ? Has Jupiter then smitten him with his thunders ? By my troth , I think so ; for , oh Jupiter ! he is in a lethargy just like one dead .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1075
sections.original: ibo et cognoscam , quisquis est . Amphitruo híc quidem est érus meus .
sections.translation: I ’ ll approach , that I may learn who it is . ( She advances .) Surely , this is my master Amphitryon . ( Calls aloud .) Ho ! Amphitryon !

sections.label: Section 5.1.1076b
sections.original: Perii .
sections.translation: I ’ m dead .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1076c
sections.original: Súrge .
sections.translation: Arise .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1076d
sections.original: Interii .
sections.translation: I ’ m slain outright .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1076e
sections.original: Cédo manum .
sections.translation: Give me your hand .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1076f
sections.original: Quis mé tenet ?
sections.translation: Who is it that has hold of me ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1077
sections.original: Tua Bromia ancilla .
sections.translation: Bromia , your maid-servant .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1077b
sections.original: Totus timeo , íta me increpuit Iuppiter .
sections.translation: I tremble all over , to such a degree has Jove pealed against me . And no otherwise is it than if I had come hither from Acheron . But why have you come out of the house ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1080
sections.original: in aedibus , tu ubi habitas . nimia mira vidi . vae mihi ,
sections.translation: in the house where you yourself dwell , have I seen astounding prodigies . Woe to me , Amphitryon ; even now do my senses fail me to such a degree .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1081b
sections.original: Agedum expedi :
sections.translation: Come now , tell me ; do you know me to be your master Amphitryon ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1082b
sections.original: Scio .
sections.translation: I do know it .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1082c
sections.original: Vide etiam nunc .
sections.translation: Look even once again .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1082d
sections.original: Scio .
sections.translation: I do know it .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1083
sections.original: Haec sola sanam mentem gestat meorum familiarium .
sections.translation: She alone of all my household has a sane mind .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1084
sections.original: Immo omnes sani sunt profecto .
sections.translation: Nay but , really , they are all of them sane .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1084b
sections.original: At me uxor insanum facit
sections.translation: But my wife causes me to be insane

sections.label: Section 5.1.1085
sections.original: suis foedis factis .
sections.translation: by her own shameful practices .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1085b
sections.original: At ego faciam , tu idem ut aliter praedices ,
sections.translation: But I ’ ll make you , Amphitryon , to be holding other language ; that you may understand that your wife is dutiful and chaste , upon that subject I will in a few words discover some tokens and some proofs . In the first place of all , Alcmena has given birth to two sons .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1089
sections.original: Ain tu , geminos ?
sections.translation: Two , say you ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1089b
sections.original: Geminos .
sections.translation: Two .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1089c
sections.original: Di me servant .
sections.translation: The Gods preserve me !

sections.label: Section 5.1.1089d
sections.original: Sine me dicere ,
sections.translation: Allow me to speak ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.1090
sections.original: ut scias tibi tuaeque uxori deos esse omnis propitios .
sections.translation: that you may know that all the Deities are propitious to yourself and to your wife .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1091
sections.original: Loquere .
sections.translation: Say on .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1091b
sections.original: Postquam parturire hodie uxor occepit tua ,
sections.translation: After that , this day , your wife began to be in labour , when the pangs of childbirth came on , as is the custom with women in travail , she invoked the immortal Gods to give her aid , with washed hands [*] and with covered head . Then forthwith it thundered

sections.label: Section 5.1.1095
sections.original: sonitu maxumo ; aedes primo ruere rebamur tuas .
sections.translation: with most tremendous crash . At first we thought that your house was falling ; all your house shone bright , as though it had been made of gold .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1097
sections.original: Quaeso absolvito hinc me extemplo , quando satis deluseris .
sections.translation: Prithee , relieve me quickly from this , since you have kept me long enough in suspense . What happened then ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1098b
sections.original: Dum haec aguntur , interea uxorem tuam
sections.translation: While these things were passing , meanwhile , not one of us heard your wife groaning or complaining ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.1100
sections.original: ita profecto sine dolore peperit .
sections.translation: and thus , in fact , without pain was she delivered .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1100b
sections.original: Iam istuc gaudeo ,
sections.translation: Then do I rejoice at this , whatever she has merited at my hands .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1101b
sections.original: Mitte ista atque haec quae dicam accipe .
sections.translation: Leave that alone , and hear these things which I shall tell you . After she was delivered , she bade us wash the babes ; we commenced to do so . But that child which I washed , how stout , how very powerful he is ; and not a person was there , able to wrap him in the swaddling-clothes .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1105
sections.original: Nimia mira memoras ; si istaec vera sunt , divinitus
sections.translation: Most wondrous things you tell of . If these things are true , I do not apprehend but that succour has been brought to my wife from heaven .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1107
sections.original: Magis iam faxo mira dices . postquam in cunas conditust ,
sections.translation: Now shall I make you own to things more wondrous still . After he was laid in the cradle , two immense crested serpents glided down through the skylight ; instantly they both reared their heads .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1109b
sections.original: Ei mihi .
sections.translation: Ah me !

sections.label: Section 5.1.1110
sections.original: Ne pave . sed angues oculis omnis circumvisere .
sections.translation: Be not dismayed — but the serpents began to gaze upon all around . After they beheld the children , quickly they made towards the cradle ; I , fearing for the children , alarmed for myself , going backwards , began to draw and pull the cradle to and fro , and so much the more fiercely did the serpents pursue . After that one of the children caught sight of the serpents ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.1115
sections.original: citus e cunis exilit , facit recta in anguis impetum :
sections.translation: he quickly leapt from the cradle , straightway he made an attack upon them , ana suddenly he grasped them , one in each hand .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1117
sections.original: Mira memoras , nimis formidolosum facinus praedicas ;
sections.translation: You tell of wondrous things ; a very fearful exploit do you relate ; for at your words horror steals upon the limbs of wretched me . What happened then ? Say on .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1119b
sections.original: Puer ambo angues enicat .
sections.translation: The child slew both the serpents . While these things are passing , in a loud voice there calls upon your wife —

sections.label: Section 5.1.1121
sections.original: Quis homo ?
sections.translation: What person —?

sections.label: Section 5.1.1121b
sections.original: Summus imperator divom atque hominum Iuppiter .
sections.translation: Jupiter , the supreme Ruler of Gods and men . He said that he had secretly enjoyed Alcmena in his embraces , and that he was his own son who had overcome those serpents ; the other , he said , was your child .

sections.label: Section 5.1.1124b
sections.original: Pol me haud paenitet ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I am not sorry

sections.label: Section 5.1.1125
sections.original: si licet boni dimidium mihi dividere cum Iove .
sections.translation: if I am allowed to take my half of a blessing in partnership with Jupiter . Go home , and bid the sacred vessels to be at once prepared for me , that with many victims I may seek my peace with supreme Jove . I will apply to Tiresias [*] the soothsayer , and consult him what he considers ought to be done ; at the same time I ’ ll relate to him this matter just as it has happened . ( It thunders .)

sections.label: Section 5.1.1130
sections.original: sed quid hoc ? quam valide tonuit . di , obsecro vostram fidem .
sections.translation: But what means this ? How dreadfully it thunders ! Ye Gods , your mercy , I do entreat .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1131
sections.original: Bono ánimo es , adsum auxílio , Amphitruo , tíbi et tuis :
sections.translation: Be of good cheer , Amphitryon ; I am come to thy aid : thou hast nothing to fear ; all diviners and soothsayers let alone . What is to be , and what has past , I will tell thee ; and so much better than they can , inasmuch as I am Jupiter .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1135
sections.original: primum omnium Alcumenae usuram corporis
sections.translation: First of all , I have made loan of the person of Alcmena , and have caused her to be pregnant with a son . Thou , too , didst cause her to be pregnant , when thou didst set out upon the expedition ; at one birth has she brought forth the two together . One of these , the one that is sprung from my parentage ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.1140
sections.original: suis factis te immortali adficiet gloria .
sections.translation: shall bless thee [*] with deathless glory by his deeds . Do thou return with Alcmena to your former affection ; she merits not that thou shouldst impute it to her as her blame ; by my power has she been compelled thus to act . I now return to the heavens .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1144
sections.original: Fáciam ita ut iubés et te oro , prómissa ut servés tua .
sections.translation: I ’ ll do as thou dost command me ; and I entreat thee to keep thy promises .

sections.label: Section 5.2.1145
sections.original: ibo ad uxorem intro , missum facio Teresiam senem .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in-doors to my wife . I dismiss the aged Tiresias from my thoughts .
